items.title: Mission
items.desc: A self-hostable URL shortener that small teams can stand up on their own domain in an afternoon. Bring-your-own-domain, real analytics, no third party tracking your users.

items.title: Audience — who comes first?
items.desc: We can frame v1 around different primary users. The choice cascades into FR scope, default config, and dashboard density. Pick one to optimise FOR; the others still work, just second-class.

options.name: Small teams (3-50 people, internal short-links)
options.desc: Marketing + ops sharing internal redirects on a custom domain. Light analytics, occasional bursts.
options.recommendation: OVR-1's lede already specifies "small teams"; framing v1 around them gives the dashboard a natural shape (per-link stats, recent activity feed) and keeps the $5-VPS hard constraint comfortable. Indie hackers and big-co marketing teams both still work fine downstream.

options.name: Indie hackers / single-operator
options.desc: One person running their own go-link service. Lowest analytics needs; values zero ops; appreciates the binary-and-config simplicity.

options.name: Big-company marketing teams
options.desc: More user roles, more audit, more granular analytics. Compatible with everything else; pulls the scope in a different direction.

items.title: Hard constraints
items.desc: One static binary. One config file. Must run on a $5/month VPS with 1 GB RAM. No SaaS dependencies for the redirect path — even if every other service is down, slugs still resolve.
